11: Hands-on code: Memory Management in LangChain
13: In LangChain and LangGraph, Memory is a critical component for creating intelligent
14: and natural-feeling conversational applications. It allows an AI agent to remember
15: information from past interactions, learn from feedback, and adapt to user
16: preferences. LangChain's memory feature provides the foundation for this by
17: referencing a stored history to enrich current prompts and then recording the latest
18: exchange for future use. As agents handle more complex tasks, this capability
19: becomes essential for both efficiency and user satisfaction.
20: Short-Term Memory: This is thread-scoped, meaning it tracks the ongoing
21: conversation within a single session or thread. It provides immediate context, but a full
22: history can challenge an LLM's context window, potentially leading to errors or poor
23: performance. LangGraph manages short-term memory as part of the agent's state,
24: which is persisted via a checkpointer, allowing a thread to be resumed at any time.
25: Long-Term Memory: This stores user-specific or application-level data across
26: sessions and is shared between conversational threads. It is saved in custom
27: "namespaces" and can be recalled at any time in any thread. LangGraph provides
28: stores to save and recall long-term memories, enabling agents to retain knowledge
30: LangChain provides several tools for managing conversation history, ranging from
31: manual control to automated integration within chains.
